title: Crafting Your Argument: Structure, Types, and Talking to Your Audience

questions.question: What does "one-sided argument" mean?
options: an argument that only presents one point of view or side of an issue.
an argument that presents both the 'for' (pros) and 'against' (cons) points of view on a topic.
serious and official language, avoiding slang or overly casual words.
a way of writing that uses pronouns like 'he', 'she', 'it', or 'they', rather than 'I' or 'you'.
questions.correct_answer: an argument that only presents one point of view or side of an issue.
questions.explanation: "one-sided argument" means: an argument that only presents one point of view or side of an issue.

questions.question: What does "balanced argument" mean?
options: an argument that presents both the 'for' (pros) and 'against' (cons) points of view on a topic.
serious and official language, avoiding slang or overly casual words.
an argument that only presents one point of view or side of an issue.
a way of writing that uses pronouns like 'he', 'she', 'it', or 'they', rather than 'I' or 'you'.
questions.correct_answer: an argument that presents both the 'for' (pros) and 'against' (cons) points of view on a topic.
questions.explanation: "balanced argument" means: an argument that presents both the 'for' (pros) and 'against' (cons) points of view on a topic.

questions.question: Fill in the blank: Argument texts aim to inform and ______ with a clear point of view.
questions.explanation: The full sentence is: "Argument texts aim to inform and persuade with a clear point of view."

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE about this topic?
options: Use formal language, third-person perspective, and effective connectives.
A strong argument has a clear structure: title, introduction, body paragraphs, and conclusion.
Argument texts aim to inform and persuade with a clear point of view.
Arguments can be one-sided (one view) or balanced (for and against).
questions.correct_answer: Arguments can be one-sided (one view) or balanced (for and against).
questions.explanation: Correct! "Arguments can be one-sided (one view) or balanced (for and against)." is one of the key things to remember.

an argument that presents both the 'for' (pros) and 'against' (cons) points of view on a topic.
an argument that only presents one point of view or side of an issue.
serious and official language, avoiding slang or overly casual words.
